questions.prompt:
A **1-meter metal bar** is heated along its length. At steady state, the temperature $u(x)$ along the bar (where $x$ is position in meters, $0 \le x \le 1$) satisfies

$$-\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} = f(x),$$

where $f(x)$ describes how much heat is added along the bar. The **ends are held at $0^\circ$C**, so $u(0)=u(1)=0$.

An engineer places **five equally spaced sensors inside** the bar (not at the ends), uses step size $h=1/6$, and assumes **constant** heating $f(x)=36$. That leads to a linear system $A\mathbf{u}=\mathbf{b}$ with $\mathbf{b}=[1,1,1,1,1]^{\top}$. After solving for the approximate temperatures at the five interior points, **what is the temperature at the center interior sensor, $u_3$?**

llmContext.overviewText: Classic 1D heat model on a rod: second derivative of temperature relates to heat source; boundaries fixed at zero. Interior unknowns come from a finite-difference stencil that builds a tridiagonal system.

nodes.title: What is being modeled
nodes.reference: Five interior samples mean six segments along the bar, so $h=1/6$. Boundaries $u_0=u_6=0$ are known; the five unknowns are temperatures in between.

nodes.title: From PDE to linear equations
nodes.reference: Approximate $u''$ with second differences; each interior row ties a point to its neighbors. With $f=36$ and $h^2=1/36$, the right-hand side becomes $1$ at each interior equation.

nodes.title: After solving
nodes.reference: The physical picture is symmetric: same heating, cold ends, so the hottest spot should be near the middle. The center interior value is the maximum among the five.

llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt:
Three **machine mounts** use different spring layouts. Each mount’s stiffness is a $2\times 2$ positive definite matrix $K$: it tells how strongly the mount resists motion in two directions.

The **condition number** $\kappa = \lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$ compares stiffness in the stiffest vs. softest direction. A **much larger $\kappa$** means the mount is very uneven—small errors in applied force can produce **very uneven** motion, so the system is **more sensitive**.

Here are the three stiffness matrices:

$$K_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 100 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix},\quad K_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix},\quad K_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}.$$

Their condition numbers are $\kappa_1 = 50$, $\kappa_2 = 3$, and $\kappa_3 \approx 5.828$. **Which mount is the most sensitive** to uneven applied forces (largest $\kappa$)?

llmContext.overviewText: Eigenvalues of a symmetric positive definite $K$ are the principal stiffnesses; condition number is their ratio. Bigger ratio means more directional imbalance.

nodes.title: Mount 1
nodes.reference: $K_1$ is diagonal: eigenvalues are $100$ and $2$, so $\kappa_1=50$—one direction is fifty times stiffer than the other.

nodes.title: Mounts 2 and 3
nodes.reference: Solve $\det(K-\lambda I)=0$ for $K_2$ and $K_3$ to get eigenvalues, then form $\kappa$. Both are much smaller than $50$.

llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt:
A **phone camera** reads two raw sensor channels, $x_1$ and $x_2$. The imaging chip turns them into two outputs: **brightness** (first) and **color balance** (second). Each output is a **linear mix** of the raw channels.

With the mixing matrix below, the transformation is $\mathbf{y} = A\mathbf{x}$ where $\mathbf{x}=[x_1,x_2]^{\top}$:

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{pmatrix}.$$

(First row = how brightness depends on channels; second row = color balance.)

For a particular shot, $\mathbf{x}=[5,3]^{\top}$. **What is the brightness** (the first component of $\mathbf{y}$)?

llmContext.overviewText: Matrix–vector multiply: first row of $A$ dotted with $\mathbf{x}$ gives the first output.

nodes.title: Big picture
nodes.reference: $A$ is invertible here, so you could recover raw channels from processed outputs in principle; column space is all of $\mathbb{R}^2$.

llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt:
A simple **campus app** model tracks daily **views** and **shares**. If today’s counts are written as a column vector, tomorrow’s predicted counts are obtained by multiplying by a fixed matrix $A$:

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}.$$

The **eigenvalues** of $A$ describe how certain special “patterns” of views vs. shares grow or stay steady over time. **What are the eigenvalues $\lambda$ of $A$?**

llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt:
Two **subway stations** share riders. Let $\mathbf{x}_k$ be the vector of passenger counts at the two stations **after $k$ days**. A planning model says **each day’s counts** are obtained from the previous day by

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = A\mathbf{x}_k,\quad A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}.$$

Today (day $0$) the counts are $\mathbf{x}_0=[10,5]^{\top}$. Using diagonalization $A=PDP^{-1}$ with eigenvalues $5$ and $2$, **what is the number of passengers at station A**—the **first entry of $\mathbf{x}_3$**—after **three** days?

nodes.title: Eigenvalues and $P$
nodes.reference: Eigenvalues $5$ and $2$; build $P$ from eigenvectors, compute $P^{-1}$, then apply $D^3$.

llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt:
A **bridge joint** is modeled so that elastic energy depends on a small displacement vector $\mathbf{x}$ through a **stiffness matrix** $K$:

$$K = \begin{pmatrix} 6 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}.$$

Engineers want to know if $K$ is **positive definite** (energy is a “bowl” opening upward, stable equilibrium at $\mathbf{0}$). Using **Sylvester’s criterion**—check that **every leading principal minor** is **positive**—**is $K$ positive definite?**
options: Yes — both leading minors are positive
No — the matrix is positive semidefinite only

llmContext.overviewText: For $2\times 2$ symmetric $K$: first minor is top-left entry; second is $\det(K)$. Both must be $>0$ for positive definite.
llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt:
A **water network** with three linked reservoirs is adjusted using three control variables $(x_1,x_2,x_3)$. Conservation and pump rules give this linear system for the **net adjustments** that must hold simultaneously:

$$\begin{aligned} 4x_1+2x_2+x_3 &= 7 \\ 6x_1+7x_2+3x_3 &= 18 \\ 2x_1+x_2+5x_3 &= 12 \end{aligned}$$

You solve it by **LU factorization**: factor $A=LU$, solve $L\mathbf{c}=\mathbf{b}$, then $U\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{c}$. **What is $x_3$?**

llmContext.overviewText: Standard $LU$ workflow on $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$: forward substitution on $L$, then back substitution on $U$.

nodes.title: What to expect
nodes.reference: Last equation in $U$ determines $x_3$ first in back substitution.

llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt:
A **sensor network** has $n$ nodes. Each node’s correction $x_i$ is linked to measurements $b_i$ by $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$, where $A$ is the $n\times n$ matrix with **$n$ on the diagonal** and **$-1$ everywhere off the diagonal** (each node resists changing on its own but is pulled toward neighbors).

Someone guesses that $A^{-1}$ has a **simple pattern**: on the diagonal a constant $c$, off the diagonal all $1$s, and the **whole matrix** scaled by $\dfrac{1}{n+1}$. **What value of $c$ makes $AA^{-1}=I$?** (Hint: look at one **off-diagonal** entry of the product.)

llmContext.overviewText: Multiply row 1 of $A$ by column 2 of the proposed $A^{-1}$ (ignoring the overall $1/(n+1)$ scale first, then enforce zero).

nodes.title: Off-diagonal entry
nodes.reference: You get a linear equation in $c$ involving $n$ and the count of $-1$ terms; solving gives $c=2$.

llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt:
A **bakery** blends four ingredient mixes (in kg) $x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4$ to hit targets for three products. The requirements form $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ with

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & 5 & 4 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix},\quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 8 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}.$$

You row-reduce the **augmented matrix** $[A\,|\,\mathbf{b}]$ to echelon form. **Which variable is free** (does **not** correspond to a pivot column)?

llmContext.overviewText: Pivot columns identify basic variables; a non-pivot column means that variable is free. Three equations, four unknowns, full row rank $\Rightarrow$ one free variable.

nodes.title: After elimination
nodes.reference: Typical outcome: pivots in columns 1, 2, and 4; column 3 has no pivot, so $x_3$ is free.

llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt:
A **delivery van**’s fuel use $y$ (liters) is recorded at several distances $x$ (km). You fit a **straight line** $y = c + dx$ to the data. The points are $(10,3)$, $(20,5)$, $(30,7)$, and $(40,8)$—not perfectly on a line, so you use **least squares**.

Set up $A\mathbf{z}=\mathbf{b}$ with $\mathbf{z}=[c,d]^{\top}$ and solve the **normal equations** $A^{\top}A\mathbf{z}=A^{\top}\mathbf{b}$. **What is the best-fit slope $d$** (liters per extra km)?

llmContext.overviewText: Each data point gives one row $[1, x_i]$ in $A$; $\mathbf{b}$ holds observed $y_i$. Normal equations yield a $2\times 2$ system for $c$ and $d$.

nodes.title: Meaning of $d$
nodes.reference: $d$ estimates extra liters burned per additional kilometer.

llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.
